issues.title: SCurve chart missing ARIA live region and keyboard data point announcements
issues.description: The SVG chart has basic aria-label but hovered data points are not announced to screen readers. The keyboard navigation (ArrowLeft/Right) changes hovered state but never announces the value via aria-live, making the chart unusable for assistive tech users. Also missing role='group' on data point clusters.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/budget/SCurve.tsx, in the SCurve component: Add an aria-live='polite' region outside the SVG that announces the currently hovered/focused data point. BEFORE: Keyboard ArrowLeft/Right updates `hovered` state but no screen reader announcement occurs. AFTER: A visually hidden <div aria-live='polite'> renders text like 'September 2023: Planned $6.5M, Actual $6.2M' whenever `hovered` state changes. Also add aria-label attributes to each circle data point element with its month and value. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: computeCurrentPaymentDue has incorrect penny-level rounding for AIA G702 calculations
issues.description: The function computes `previousWork` and `currentWork` using inconsistent rounding. `previousWork = Math.round(scheduledValue * prevPctComplete) / 100` divides by 100 which is a percentage conversion, not penny rounding. If prevPctComplete is already a decimal (0.45), multiplying then dividing by 100 gives wrong results. If it's a whole number (45), the division is correct but workThisPeriod rounding is applied after subtraction causing potential penny drift. AIA G702 requires exact-to-the-penny calculations.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/payApplications.ts, in the computeCurrentPaymentDue function: Clarify and fix the percentage interpretation. BEFORE: `previousWork = Math.round(scheduledValue * prevPctComplete) / 100` — this divides by 100 after rounding, which is ambiguous about whether prevPctComplete is 0.45 or 45. workThisPeriod rounding happens after subtraction causing penny errors. AFTER: Add a JSDoc comment specifying prevPctComplete and currentPctComplete are decimal fractions (0.0 to 1.0). Change calculation to: `const previousWork = Math.round(scheduledValue * prevPctComplete * 100) / 100` (round to cents). Change `const currentWork = Math.round(scheduledValue * currentPctComplete * 100) / 100`. Change `const workThisPeriod = Math.round((currentWork - previousWork) * 100) / 100`. This ensures all intermediate values are rounded to cents before further arithmetic, matching AIA G702 requirements. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: WaterfallChart and Treemap have no responsive breakpoint handling for mobile
issues.description: WaterfallChart uses a fixed 200px height and horizontal flex layout with no mobile adaptation. On a 375px screen, four bars at flex:1 are ~80px each — too narrow for readable labels. No stacked/vertical layout for mobile. Touch targets on bars have no minimum size. The Treemap has isMobile detection but WaterfallChart does not.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/budget/WaterfallChart.tsx, in the WaterfallChart component: Add mobile responsive behavior. BEFORE: Fixed horizontal 4-bar layout with flex:1 and 200px height, no window width detection, labels truncate on small screens. AFTER: Add a useState/useEffect for isMobile (window.innerWidth < 768) with resize listener (debounced). When isMobile is true: change the bars container to flex-direction column, set each bar to a horizontal layout (height: 48px minimum for touch targets), and show value labels to the right of bars instead of above. Set min-height to 320px on mobile. Ensure bar labels use fontSize.xs on mobile. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: BudgetUpload silently fails on malformed Excel rows without user feedback
issues.description: The parseNumber helper and AI mapping silently return 0 or null for unrecognized values. If a user uploads a budget spreadsheet with text in amount columns (e.g. 'TBD', 'N/A'), every row maps to $0 budget with no warning. The user sees a successful import of zero-dollar line items. There is no validation summary showing skipped/problematic rows before confirming import.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/budget/BudgetUpload.tsx, in the component after the file parsing logic: Add a validation warnings array. BEFORE: parseNumber returns 0 for non-numeric values and rows are silently included with $0 amounts. No warning shown to user before import confirmation. AFTER: Create a `warnings: string[]` state array. In the parsing loop, when parseNumber encounters a non-numeric value (NaN after parsing), push a warning like `Row ${index + 1}: '${rawValue}' in budget column is not a number, defaulting to $0`. After parsing, if warnings.length > 0, display an amber warning box above the preview table showing the count ('3 rows had issues') with an expandable list of individual warnings. Add a CSS class or inline style with backgroundColor matching colors.statusPending at 10% opacity. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: EarnedValueDashboard loading skeleton count mismatches final card count
issues.description: The loading skeleton renders 6 placeholder cards in a 3-column grid, but the actual metrics array contains more than 6 items (CPI, SPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, CV, SV, and potentially more based on the truncated code). This causes a visible layout shift when data loads — skeleton shows 2 rows of 3, but actual content may show 3+ rows. PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA mandates skeleton geometry must match final layout with zero CLS.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/budget/EarnedValueDashboard.tsx, in the EarnedValueDashboard component: Fix the skeleton count to match the actual metrics rendered. BEFORE: Loading state renders `Array.from({ length: 6 })` skeleton cards. The actual metrics array likely has 7-8 items (CPI, SPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, Cost Variance, Schedule Variance, and possibly TCPI). AFTER: Count the actual number of EVMetric objects in the `metrics` array (check the full array after the truncated code). Update the skeleton to render exactly that many placeholders: `Array.from({ length: ACTUAL_COUNT })`. Also ensure the grid template columns match between skeleton and content states — if content uses a different grid on mobile, skeleton should too. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's financial module lacks earned value analysis (CPI/SPI) entirely — their budget page shows only original vs actual with no EVM metrics. SiteSync's EarnedValueDashboard and computeCurrentPaymentDue functions already exceed Procore on financial depth. However, Procore's pay application workflow is battle-tested with pixel-perfect AIA G702/G703 PDF output, while SiteSync's pay app calculations have the penny rounding bug identified above which would be a dealbreaker for any CFO. Sage 300 CRE integration is Procore's top-requested feature too, making P0-1 from the roadmap a legitimate competitive differentiator if shipped correctly.
summary: The Financial Engine module is the strongest conceptual differentiator in SiteSync — earned value analysis, AIA G702 calculations, waterfall charts, treemaps, and S-curves represent real construction financial intelligence that Procore lacks. However, execution has critical gaps: the pay application calculation has an ambiguous percentage/rounding issue that would produce wrong payment amounts (a P0 bug for any GC), the charts lack accessibility for screen readers (violating PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA global standards), mobile responsiveness is absent on key visualizations, and the budget upload flow silently swallows bad data. The EV dashboard skeleton mismatch causes layout shift. Fixing these five issues — penny-accurate AIA math, chart accessibility, mobile layouts, upload validation feedback, and skeleton geometry — would lift the module from 49 to the 65-70 range and make the financial depth actually trustworthy for CFO use.
